coredata.dc:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots through liquid phase exfoliation method
coredata.dc:creator: Zdrazil, L.
Zahradnicek, R.
Mohan, R.
Sedlacek, P.
Nejdl, Lukas
Schmiedova, V.
Pospisil, J.
Horak, M.
Weiter, M.
Zmeskal, O.
Hubalek, J.
coredata.dc:description: Here we report the preparation of low defect blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through microwave expansion and liquid phase exfoliation (LPE) of graphite flakes with a high preparation yield of 0.63 g of GQDs per 1 g of original graphite substrate. The maximum emission in the prepared GQDs occurs at 467 nm for 370 nm excitation. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs was measured as 3.4%. Prepared GQDs are composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements. They have high percentage (86%) of C-C/C=C, indicating few structural defects of the graphitic core, due to lesser number of edge- or surface-located oxygenated functional groups. This preparation technique, unlike others, does not require additional chemical components and is energetically undemanding. Furthermore, the reported method is also suitable for scalable preparation of quantum structures from other two-dimensional layered materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon material
Nanoparticles
Liquid phase exfoliation
Microwave expansion
Luminescence

Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge assistance from the Sustainability and Development REG LO1211 project addressed to the Materials Research Centre at FCH BUT. The research also received support from the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic under project CEITEC 2020 ( LQ1601 ). This research has also been financially supported by the Specific University Research grant of Brno University of Technology , FEKT/STI-J-18-5354 . The investigation described herein was funded by the Czech Ministry of Education Within National Sustainability Program under grant LO1401 . For the actual research, the infrastructure of the SIX Center was used. Further, the paper was supported from project No. FEKT-S-17-3934, Utilization of novel findings in micro and nanotechnologies for complex electronic circuits and sensor applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images comparing (a) graphite before and (b) after the microwave expansion; (c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs with size distribution; (d) AFM image of GQDs on silicon substrate. The inset shows height distribution of GQDs.
(a) Schematics of formation of GQDs from graphite flakes; (b) GQDs dispersion under daylight and (c) under 365 nm light.

(a) Absorption spectrum of the prepared GQDs; (b) excitation and emission spectra of GQDs, excited at different wavelengths; (c) normalized emission spectra with excitation wavelength, (d) variation of emission peak position and their full width at half maxima (FWHM) of the emission peak with excitation wavelength.
Electron energy loss spectrum of a GQD on an amorphous carbon membrane showing the excitonic excitation at 2.9 eV (corresponding wavelength 427 nm).
The XPS spectra of the GQDs: (a) survey scan; (b) high resolution spectrum of C 1s; (c) high resolution spectrum of O 1s; (d) high resolution spectrum of N 1s.

(a) Raman spectra of GQDs and graphite; (b) detail on D and G band. (c) HRTEM image of a GQDs showing interplanar spacing of 0.242 nm, red arrows show the fracturing of graphene structure to form GQDs and yellow circle show the outer boundary of the GQD, area enclosed in the green lines show the defect free GQD; (d) The proposed formation and molecular structure model of the GQDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots through liquid phase exfoliation method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Brno University of Technology, Purkynova 118, Brno 612 00, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, Brno University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Mendel University in Brno

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report the preparation of low defect blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through microwave expansion and liquid phase exfoliation (LPE) of graphite flakes with a high preparation yield of 0.63 g of GQDs per 1 g of original graphite substrate. The maximum emission in the prepared GQDs occurs at 467 nm for 370 nm excitation. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs was measured as 3.4%. Prepared GQDs are composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements. They have high percentage (86%) of C-C/C=C, indicating few structural defects of the graphitic core, due to lesser number of edge- or surface-located oxygenated functional groups. This preparation technique, unlike others, does not require additional chemical components and is energetically undemanding. Furthermore, the reported method is also suitable for scalable preparation of quantum structures from other two-dimensional layered materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their exceptional chemical and physical properties the graphene-based materials are promising for state-of-the-art nanodevices. Graphene is regarded as a semi-metal having zero band gap [1]. Some applications, however, require a tunable band gap; an applicable solution then consists in restricting graphene electrons by transforming the shape from a 2D structure to 0D quantum dots. This transformation leads to quantization of energy levels and creation of band gap-like structure. Therefore, the excitons with non-infinite Bohr diameter appear in these graphene fragments and can be observed in luminescence measurements [2]. Nanoscale graphene sheets showing photoluminescence (PL) are called graphene quantum dots (GQDs). PL in GQDs depends mainly on their size and bonded functional groups. Various methods like hydrothermal cutting, solvothermal cutting, electrochemical cutting, nanolithography, microwave-assisted cutting, ultrasonic shearing and ultrasonic-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation have been reported for preparing GQDs [3–8]. Most of these methods are based on oxidation or reduction of carbon nanomaterials [3–5]. Graphite is one of the cheapest and most common precursors for the preparation GQDs as well as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). However, the synthesis of GQDs using GO or rGO prepared from natural graphite usually involves complicated and time-consuming procedures. Until now, there are only few reports on the preparation of GQDs directly from graphite, due to the great difficulties and challenges in exfoliating and cleaving the monolayers of graphite into small dots on the nanometric scale. Other techniques of GQDs preparation require high energy costs and expansive deposition procedures [6,8]. Finding a one-step, low-cost, technologically easy-to-use and environmentally friendly method, which uses commonly available carbon materials as a precursor, can be considered as a challenge that could lead to the large-scale production of GQDs.

Recently, the liquid phase exfoliation (LPE) of 2D materials has drawn considerable interest [9,10] due to its scalability. LPE via ultrasonication is one of the most promising ways to produce 2D nanosheets with various advantages like low cost production, simplicity in operation, or minimum environmental impact [11]. During the long LPE process the graphite is exfoliated to graphene layers and many of them are of very small size (quantum size). GQDs has been prepared through LPE using low defect nano-graphite powder and highly defected acetylene carbon powder [12]. Both these precursors used are expansive and already in the nanosize. These precursors already have some type of edge or surface defects. Since, the fluorescence in GQDs is supposed to be related to their surface and defects, therefore, it will be highly desired to used precursor without defects. Then the prepared GQDs can be studied for the type and amount of defects with respect to their luminescence. In this paper we have used expandable natural graphite flakes which were expanded in a microwave oven. The expanded graphite was then exfoliated through LPE via ultrasonication in NMP solution. This LPE of expanded graphite resulted in quantum sized graphene, which was collected from the exfoliated graphene suspension called GQDs. Compared to other preparation methods of GQDs, this synthesis does not require carbon nanomaterials as precursors (nano-graphite [12], graphene/graphene oxide [3,5,8,9], but natural graphite flakes. This process of the preparation GQDs by combining microwave expansion and LPE is facile, inexpensive and scalable. Owing to these features, the GQDs prepared by presented method could be used in various applications as components of new generation solar cells or in biological sensing.

The expandable graphite flakes with particle size > 300 µm (Graphite Týn s.r.o. Czech Republic) were expanded by placing them in a domestic microwave oven (750 W) for 1 min. These expanded graphite flakes were then suspended in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) with the concentration of 3.32 mg/ml. The resulting suspension was then exfoliated in an ultrasonic disintegrator (DSA 50-GL1-1.6l) using output power of 50 W and frequency of 40 kHz for 9 h. This sonication was followed by overnight sedimentation and the withdrawal of the supernatant. The residual supernatant was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate all non-exfoliated graphite particles. The last step within the preparation consisted in filtering the supernatant through a 200 nm polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and regenerated cellulose (RC) membrane filter.

The SEM morphologies of the graphite flakes before and after expansion were examined by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FEI VERIOS 460L). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and EELS measurement were done on transmission electron microscope FEI Titan Themis 60-300 cubed. The size and height of the GQDs were measured with atomic force microscopy (AFM, ICON-SPM). The emission spectra of the prepared GQDs were measured with a FLUOROLOG instrument at various excitation lengths between 300 and 800 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using Axis Supra by Kratos with AlKα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. Raman spectra were measured using commercial Renishaw inVia Raman Spectrometer with 532 nm single-mode diode laser as the excitation source. Thermogravimetric measurement was performed on thermogravimetric analyzer Q5000 (TA Instruments). At first, the dispersion was heated in nitrogen atmosphere from 25 °C to 200 °C with the heating rate of 5 °C per minute. Subsequently, the sample was maintained at 200 °C for 30 min to complete the evaporation of the solvent.

Natural graphite flake consists of millions of stacked graphene layers. For preparation of the graphene quantum dots the graphite flakes are expanded via microwave irradiation. Microwave irradiation weakens/breaks the van der Waals interlayer binding between different graphite layers resulting in the expansion process [13]. Microwave irradiation resulted in large expansion of graphite flakes as shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b) where a graphite flake before and after microwave expansion are presented. The NMP induces intercalation of molecules of the solvent between the graphene sheets as well attachment of organic molecules on their surface and edges. During ultrasonication, shear forces and cavitation, i.e. the growth and collapse of the micrometer-sized bubbles or voids in liquids due to pressure fluctuations, act on the expanded graphite flakes and induce exfoliation. During this process, oxygen and nitrogen atoms form bonds on graphene surface and edges [12]. The prolonged sonication at low energy results in exfoliation of ultra-small graphene sheets and in binding of oxygen and nitrogen with carbon atoms on edges and on the surface. Fig. 1(c) shows the TEM image of GQDs with size distribution.

GQDs have sheet like structures [14] and the lateral size of GQDs is about 6 nm, which is comparable with previous reports [15,16]. Unlike others preparation processes, this exfoliation in NMP does not require oxidation or reducing agents [3,4,17]. Fig. 1(d) shows AFM image of GQDs on a silicon substrate. The distribution of the AFM height is shown in the inset of Fig. 1(d). The average height of the GQDs is about 3.6 nm, which corresponds to structures up to few layers of graphene.

The schematic of preparation of GQDs from graphite flakes is shown in Fig. 2(a). The prepared suspension of GQDs in NMP, under daylight and 365 nm light is shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c). In daylight, the dispersion appeared yellowish, but under 365 nm UV light, GQDs show blue luminescence. The production yield of the GQDs was examined via thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S1). The production yield found to be 21 mg, which corresponds to 63% of the weight of original graphite flakes. Nevertheless, the true graphite utilization efficiency is lower since the weight of GQDs is affected by the presence of surface-and edge-bound functional groups (mainly oxygen and nitrogen containing ones), introduced into the structure of GQDs during the preparation process. The representative absorption spectrum of graphene quantum dots is presented in Fig. 3(a). The absorption spectra do not show a maximum in VIS region but exhibit a broad absorption tail; such an effect can be caused by the polydispersity of the GQDs [18]. It was noticed that many GQDs exhibit local absorption maxima in the range of 270–390 nm, which can be associate with n→π* transition. This phenomenon appears to be associated with dopants, structural defects and surface states [19].

GQDs prepared by presented methods show few local maxima at 269, 278 and 319 nm. This absorption corresponds to the trapping of the excited state energy due the presence of small amount of functional group bounded to the surface of GQDs [12]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of prepared GQDs at various excitation wavelengths along with the excitation spectrum are presented in Fig. 3(b). The maximum emission occurs at 467 nm under excitation at 370 nm (corresponds to peak at 370 nm in excitation spectrum) which is higher wavelength than previously reported values [15,16]. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs at 350 nm excitation was measured to be 3.4% which is more than that reported in [12] by using quinine sulfate as a standard (see Fig. S2).

The emission maxima red-shifted (from 450 to 515 nm) as the excitation wavelength increased (from 310 to 470 nm). This red shifting is more evident in the normalized emission spectra of the prepared GQDs, which is shown in Fig. 3(c). The variation of emission peak as well as the full width at half maxima (FWHM) with the excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 3(d). The emission peak position increases almost linearly with excitation wavelength before its saturation at about 470 nm. It also coincides with the fact that the emission peak shows additional peak at 480 nm when excited with 450 and 470 nm light. In the emission peak at 410 nm excitation also shows little hump at 483 nm. As reported in various works, the origin of excitation independent emission in GQDs is due to the different surface states of GQDs [3,5,7]. This means that emission peak at 483 nm corresponds to surface states which are independent of excitation wavelength [20]. The FWHM first increases (till 340 nm) and then decreases with the excitation wavelengths. This corresponds to the surface state model for the origin of fluorescence in GQDs. In general, variability in the surface states of GQDs originates from chemical modification of their structure by diverse functional groups. At the same time, the number of surface states that can be excited decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. We measured the low loss spectrum of a graphene quantum dot on an amorphous carbon membrane ( Fig. 4) in a transmission electron microscope by electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS).

The spectrum was processed as follows. First, we subtracted the zero-loss peak. Second, we performed Gaussian fitting procedure at the beginning of the inelastic signal. As the result, we addressed two peaks: the peak at 2.9 eV corresponding to the excitonic excitation in the graphene quantum dot and the peak at 4.5 eV corresponding to the surface plasmonic excitation in the amorphous carbon membrane. The detected excitonic excitation at 2.9 eV (corresponding wavelength 427 nm) is in agreement with the optical measurements discussed above. XPS analysis was performed to evaluate the chemical functionalization of the surface of GQDs. The results of XPS are plotted in Fig. 5. XPS survey scan of GQDs shows the presence of C (~ 76%), O (~ 17%) and N (~ 7%) elements (Fig. 5(a)). The deconvolution of high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 5(b)) shows that the C 1s peak consists of C-C/C˭C (~ 86%) at 284.8 eV; C-(O, N) (~ 5.3%) at 286.3 eV; C˭O (7.25%) at 287.8 eV and O-C˭C (~ 1.4%) at 288.8 eV [21]. The high percentage (86%) of C-C/C˭C indicates few structural defects of the graphitic core, due to various edge- or surface-located functional groups. The number of functional groups on the surface is also highlighted in the high-resolution O 1s and N 1s spectra. The bonding of the C atoms (in the hexagonal graphene) with O and N atoms create defects in the graphene. These defects are formed due to the carbon (in hexagonal structure) bonded with O and N atoms.

The high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 5(c)) shows that the O 1s peak consists of C˭O (~ 3.8%) at 531.08 eV; C-O (~ 93.99%) at 532.48 eV; and surface adsorbed water (~ 2.21%) at 533.68 eV [22]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 5(d)) shows that the N 1s peak consists of pyrrolic-N (~ 89.1%) at 400.6 eV and graphitic nitrogen, C3-N (~ 10.9%) at 402.6 eV [21,23]. It is possible that the edges and surfaces of GQDs are attached with NMP residual molecules. These residues created by the decomposition of NMP molecules under drying process may contribute to XPS peak at 400.6 eV. Compared to previously reported results, the prepared GQDs exhibit more graphene-like characteristics due to fewer O atoms derivatives bonded to their surfaces [3,5–7,24]. Raman spectroscopy was carried out for GQDs to examine the graphitic structure of the prepared GQDs. Raman spectrum of the graphite powder was also measured for the comparison. Fig. 6(a) displays the Raman spectrum of the GQDs and graphite. Please note that the Raman signal was interfered by the fluorescence of the GQDs excited at 532 nm laser. In the Raman spectra, the D-band known as disordered/defect band at ~ 1350 cm−1 arises from the violation of the momentum conservation law due to the formation of defects or edge states (zigzag and armchair), G-band known as graphitic band appeared at ~ 1583 cm−1 due to the in-plane lattice vibrations of sp2 carbon, and 2D-band at 2726 cm−1 is a second-order Raman process originating from the in-plane breathing-like mode of the carbon rings (Fig. 6(a)).

Another small band at 1617 cm-1, which is known as the D′ band is also observed in GQDs, but this band is absent in graphite. The relative intensity of the D-band to the G-band (I D/I G) can be used to estimate the extent of structural defects in the graphite structure [25]. The I D/I G values of the GQDs and graphite were 0.21 and 0.06, respectively. I D/I G values of the GQDs is a lot smaller compared to GQDs synthesized by cutting and organic synthesis methods [12,26–29]. The low value of I D/I G indicates low edge roughness/defects and good crystallinity in the prepared GQDs [12], which was also confirmed by XPS results. This value of I D/I G = 0.21 is even less than reported to low defect GQDs [12], which is 0.413. To find the type of defect in the prepared GQDs we have used the observation made in [30,31]. It was shown that the nature of defects in graphene can be probed by the ratio of intensities of the D and D′ bands. The I D/I D′ ratio depends on the nature of defects, with boundary (or edge) defects being characterized by I D/I D′ ~ 3.5, whereas vacancy basal plane point defects giving rise to I D/I D′ ~ 7 and sp3 defects to I D/I D′ ~ 13. The I D/I D′ value of the prepared GQDs is ~ 2.1, which consistent with edge type of defects. During the slow ultra-sonication, the planar graphitic domains are chemically initiated by the lining up of chemical functionalities (functional groups) making the graphitic domains prone to fracture as evident from Fig. 6(c). The formation of the edge defect states in the GQDs resulted in the higher I D/I G ratio of GQDs than that of graphite flakes. These edge states or defects in the graphitic structure are created by the presence of functional groups and ultrasonic damage of the graphene quantum dots [32]. Defects in the skeleton of the GQDs are visible at high resolution in HRTEM micrograph (Fig. 6(c)), where the defect free GQD is enclosed in green lines in the GQDs micrograph. Based on this observation, we propose formation and molecular structure model for better understanding (Fig. 6(d)). During the ultra-sonication the graphene domains fractured into GQDs with edge states. These results are consistent with the Raman spectrum of GQDs confirming the disorder in the structure via characteristic D-band at 1350 cm−1, a G-band observed at 1583 cm−1 and D′-band at 1617 cm-1. HRTEM image also showed GQDs has a lattice spacing of ~ 0.242 nm, which is consistent with the (1120) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon. The formation of edge/defect states may be responsible for the photoluminescence in GQDs.

Combining microwave expansion and low-energy LPE enabled us to prepare blue luminescent GQDs. Microwave treatment expands the graphite flakes and LPE through sonication separate graphene sheets. The prolong sonication results in exfoliation of ultra-small graphene sheets and creation of various surface states in exfoliated graphene sheets and thus form the GQDs. The maximum emission occurs at 467 nm under excitation at 370 nm. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs was measured as 3.4%. XPS and Raman results shows that the prepared GQDs are less defective as compared to earlier reports. The surface edge states of GQDs due to the binding of the carbon atoms with oxygen and nitrogen atoms created during sonication are mainly responsible for the luminescence in GQDs. The proposed method for GQDs preparation is simple, scalable and cost effective.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge assistance from the Sustainability and Development REG LO1211 project addressed to the Materials Research Centre at FCH BUT. The research also received support from the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic under project CEITEC 2020 (LQ1601). This research has also been financially supported by the Specific University Research grant of Brno University of Technology, FEKT/STI-J-18-5354. The investigation described herein was funded by the Czech Ministry of Education Within National Sustainability Program under grant LO1401. For the actual research, the infrastructure of the SIX Center was used. Further, the paper was supported from project No. FEKT-S-17-3934, Utilization of novel findings in micro and nanotechnologies for complex electronic circuits and sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: There is no financial or non-financial conflict interest in the subject matter or materials discussed in this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.08.017
